---
name: email
description: Read, send, reply, forward, search, and manage emails across inbox, sent, draft, and trash folders. Use when the user asks about emails, sending messages, checking inbox, or email communication.
---

# Email

Read, send, reply to, forward, search, and organize emails. Data is pre-loaded. No email account setup needed.

## Reading Emails

```bash
# List inbox emails (paginated)
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py list --folder INBOX --offset 0 --limit 10

# List sent emails
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py list --folder SENT --limit 20

# Get a specific email by ID
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py get --id abc123def456

# Get an email by its position in a folder
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py get-by-index --index 0 --folder INBOX
```

### Folders

| Folder | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `INBOX` | Received emails (default) |
| `SENT` | Sent emails |
| `DRAFT` | Draft emails |
| `TRASH` | Deleted emails |

## Sending Emails

```bash
# Send a new email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py send --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["tenant@example.com"]' \
    --subject "Rent Reminder" \
    --content "This is a reminder that rent is due on the 1st."

# Send with CC
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py send --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["tenant@example.com"]' \
    --cc '["manager@example.com"]' \
    --subject "Lease Update" \
    --content "Please review the updated terms."

# Send with attachments (pass workspace file paths as a JSON array)
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py send --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["registration@example.com"]' \
    --subject "Registration" \
    --content "Photos attached." \
    --attachment-paths '["IMG_0037.JPEG", "photo-6631.png"]'
```

## Replying & Forwarding

```bash
# Reply to an email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py reply --id abc123 --sender "me@example.com" \
    --content "Thank you for reporting this. We will address it shortly."

# Forward an email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py forward --id abc123 --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["contractor@example.com"]' \
    --content "Please see the tenant's maintenance request below."
```

## Searching

```bash
# Search emails by keyword (searches subject and content)
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py search --query "plumbing" --folder INBOX --limit 10
```

## Managing Emails

```bash
# Move an email to another folder
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py move --id abc123 --from-folder INBOX --to-folder TRASH

# Delete an email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py delete --id abc123 --folder INBOX
```

## JMAP & Mailboxes

```bash
# List mailboxes with email counts
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py mailboxes

# Get a specific mailbox
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py mailbox --id INBOX

# List JMAP-formatted emails
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py jmap-list --mailbox-id INBOX --limit 10

# Get JMAP email by ID
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py jmap-get --id abc123

# Get email thread
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py thread --id thread123
```

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "Check my inbox" | `list --folder INBOX` |
| "Any new emails?" | `list --folder INBOX --limit 5` (check unread) |
| "Read email from Maria" | `search --query "Maria"` then `get --id <id>` |
| "Send a reminder about rent" | `send --sender ... --recipients ... --subject ... --content ...` |
| "Reply to that maintenance email" | `reply --id <id> --sender ... --content ...` |
| "Forward this to the plumber" | `forward --id <id> --sender ... --recipients ... --content ...` |
| "Search for emails about leaking" | `search --query "leaking"` |
| "Delete that spam email" | `delete --id <id> --folder INBOX` |
| "What emails have I sent?" | `list --folder SENT` |
| "How many unread emails?" | `mailboxes` (check unreadEmails count) |

## Notes

- Reading an email by ID or index automatically marks it as read
- Replies auto-populate the `Re:` subject prefix and send to the original sender
- Forwards auto-populate the `Fwd:` subject prefix
- The `sender` field should be the current user's email address

---
name: calendar
description: View, create, edit, search, and delete calendar events. Filter by date range, tag, or keyword. Use when the user asks about schedules, appointments, events, reminders, or anything time-related.
---

# Calendar

View, create, edit, search, and delete calendar events. Data is pre-loaded. No setup needed.

## Viewing Events

```bash
# Get today's events
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py today

# Get events in a date range
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py range \
    --start "2026-03-01 00:00:00" --end "2026-03-31 23:59:59" \
    --limit 20

# Get a specific event by ID
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py get --id abc123def456

# Show all events (raw data)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py show --offset 0 --limit 100
```

## Filtering by Tag

```bash
# List all tags
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py tags

# Get events by tag
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py by-tag --tag "Maintenance"
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py by-tag --tag "Lease"
```

## Searching Events

```bash
# Search by keyword (searches title, description, location, attendees)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py search --query "plumbing"
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py search --query "Riverside Commons"
```

## Creating Events

```bash
# Create a new event
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py add \
    --title "Maintenance: Fix leaky faucet" \
    --start "2026-03-20 09:00:00" \
    --end "2026-03-20 11:00:00" \
    --tag "Maintenance" \
    --description "Plumber scheduled to fix kitchen faucet in unit 301" \
    --location "Unit 301, Riverside Commons" \
    --attendees '["Mike'\''s Plumbing"]'

# Minimal event (defaults: start=now, end=1hr later)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py add --title "Quick meeting"
```

### Event Fields

| Field | Flag | Required | Format |
|-------|------|----------|--------|
| Title | `--title` | No (defaults to "Event") | Text |
| Start | `--start` | No (defaults to now) | `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"` |
| End | `--end` | No (defaults to start+1hr) | `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"` |
| Tag | `--tag` | No | Text (e.g., "Maintenance", "Lease") |
| Description | `--description` | No | Text |
| Location | `--location` | No | Text |
| Attendees | `--attendees` | No | JSON array: `'["name1", "name2"]'` |

## Editing Events

```bash
# Edit an event (only specified fields are updated)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py edit --id abc123 \
    --start "2026-03-21 10:00:00" \
    --end "2026-03-21 12:00:00" \
    --description "Rescheduled to Thursday"
```

## Deleting Events

```bash
# Delete an event
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py delete --id abc123
```

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "What's on my calendar today?" | `today` |
| "Any events this week?" | `range --start "YYYY-MM-DD 00:00:00" --end "YYYY-MM-DD 23:59:59"` |
| "Show maintenance events" | `by-tag --tag "Maintenance"` |
| "When is the lease expiring for unit 301?" | `search --query "301"` or `by-tag --tag "Lease"` |
| "Schedule a plumber visit" | `add --title "..." --start "..." --end "..." --tag "Maintenance" ...` |
| "Reschedule the meeting to Thursday" | `edit --id <id> --start "..." --end "..."` |
| "Cancel that appointment" | `delete --id <id>` |
| "What tags are available?" | `tags` |
| "Search for events about Riverside" | `search --query "Riverside"` |

## Notes

- Datetime fields in responses are Unix timestamps (e.g., `1737018000.0`)
- Human-readable versions are in `start_strftime` and `end_strftime` fields
- When creating/editing, always pass datetimes as `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"` strings
- Start time must be before end time

---
name: crm
description: Manage contacts, companies, deals, leads, and engagements via the CRM platform. Use when the user asks about sales pipeline, deals, leads, client relationships, companies, engagement history, or customer relationship management.
---

# CRM

Manage contacts, companies, deals, leads, and engagements. Data is pre-loaded. No API key or setup needed.

## Contacts

```bash
# Search contacts by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-contacts --full-name "Lisa Chen"

# Get a specific contact by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-contact --id CON-1001

# Create a new contact
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-contact --full-name "Jane Doe" --email jane@example.com \
    --company-id COMP-2001 --phone 555-1234 --jobtitle "Marketing Director"

# Delete a contact
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-contact --id CON-1001
```

## Companies

```bash
# Search companies by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-companies --name "Acme"

# Get a specific company by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-company --id COMP-2001

# Create a new company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-company --name "Acme Corp" \
    --industry Technology --domain acme.com --phone 555-0100

# Delete a company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-company --id COMP-2001
```

## Deals

```bash
# Search deals by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-deals --dealname "Chen"

# Search deals by company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-deals --company-id COMP-2001

# Get a specific deal by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-deal --id DEAL-3001

# Create a new deal
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-deal --dealname "Enterprise License" \
    --dealstage qualifiedtobuy --company-id COMP-2001 --amount 50000 \
    --closedate 2026-06-30

# Update deal stage
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py update-deal-stage --id DEAL-3001 --dealstage closedwon

# Delete a deal
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-deal --id DEAL-3001
```

### Deal Stages

| Stage | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `appointmentscheduled` | Initial appointment scheduled |
| `qualifiedtobuy` | Lead qualified as potential buyer |
| `presentationscheduled` | Demo/presentation scheduled |
| `decisionmakerboughtin` | Decision maker approval obtained |
| `contractsent` | Contract sent to customer |
| `closedwon` | Deal won |
| `closedlost` | Deal lost |

## Leads

```bash
# Search leads by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-leads --full-name "Smith"

# Search leads by company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-leads --company-id COMP-2001

# Get a specific lead by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-lead --id LEAD-4001

# Create a new lead
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-lead --full-name "Bob Smith" --email bob@example.com \
    --company "Acme Corp" --rating hot --leadsource "Website"

# Delete a lead
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-lead --id LEAD-4001
```

### Lead Ratings

| Rating | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `hot` | High priority lead |
| `warm` | Medium priority lead |
| `cold` | Low priority lead |

## Engagements

```bash
# Create an email engagement
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-engagement --engagement-type EMAIL \
    --body "Follow-up on proposal" --contact-ids '["CON-1001"]' --title "Proposal Follow-up"

# Create a call engagement
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-engagement --engagement-type CALL \
    --body "Discussed pricing" --contact-ids '["CON-1001"]' --company-ids '["COMP-2001"]'

# Create a note
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-engagement --engagement-type NOTE \
    --body "Client prefers quarterly billing"

# List engagements (optionally filter)
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py list-engagements
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py list-engagements --contact-ids '["CON-1001"]'
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py list-engagements --company-ids '["COMP-2001"]'

# Delete an engagement
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-engagement --id ENG-5001
```

### Engagement Types

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `EMAIL` | Email communication |
| `CALL` | Phone call |
| `NOTE` | Internal note |

## Utility

```bash
# Show raw data
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py show --offset 0 --limit 100

# Reset to initial state
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py reset
```

## Data Entities

The CRM system holds:

- **Contacts** -- individual people (id, full_name, email, phone, company_id, jobtitle, address, etc.)
- **Companies** -- organizations (id, name, industry, contact_ids, domain, annualrevenue, numberofemployees, etc.)
- **Deals** -- sales opportunities (id, dealname, dealstage, company_id, amount, closedate, pipeline, etc.)
- **Leads** -- prospective clients (id, full_name, email, company_id, rating, leadsource, notes, etc.)
- **Engagements** -- interaction records (id, engagement_type, body, contact_ids, company_ids, title, etc.)

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "Show me all companies" | `show` or `search-companies --name ""` |
| "Find contact Lisa Chen" | `search-contacts --full-name "Lisa Chen"` |
| "What deals are in the pipeline?" | `search-deals --dealname ""` |
| "Show me the Chen Family deal" | `search-deals --dealname "Chen"` |
| "What's the status of the Alvarez deal?" | `search-deals --dealname "Alvarez"` |
| "Log a call with a client" | `create-engagement --engagement-type CALL --body "..."` |
| "Move deal to closed won" | `update-deal-stage --id DEAL-X --dealstage closedwon` |
| "Who are our hot leads?" | `search-leads` then filter by rating |
| "Show engagement history for Chen" | `list-engagements --company-ids '["COMP-X"]'` |

---
name: messaging
description: Send and receive messages, manage conversations, and search message history. Use when the user asks about messages, conversations, chats, or wants to contact someone.
---

# Messaging

Send messages, browse conversations, and search message history. Data is pre-loaded. No setup needed.

## Viewing Conversations

```bash
# List all conversations (most recently updated first)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py list

# List with pagination
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py list --offset 0 --limit 20

# Get a conversation with its messages
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py get --conversation-id CONV_ID

# Get with message pagination
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py get --conversation-id CONV_ID --offset 0 --limit 50

# Search conversations (searches title, participant names, message content)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py search --query "invoice"

# Show raw data
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py show --offset 0 --limit 100
```

## Sending Messages

```bash
# Send a message to a user (creates a new conversation if none exists)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py send --user-id USER_ID --content "Hello, how are you?"

# Send a message to an existing conversation
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py send-to --conversation-id CONV_ID \
    --content "Following up on the invoice."

# Send with an attachment
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py send --user-id USER_ID \
    --content "Please see the attached report." \
    --attachment "/path/to/report.pdf"
```

## Managing Conversations

```bash
# Create a new conversation with multiple participants
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py create \
    --participant-ids '["user_001", "user_002", "user_003"]' \
    --title "Q1 Budget Review"

# Create without a title (uses participant names)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py create \
    --participant-ids '["user_001", "user_002"]'

# Add a user to an existing conversation
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py add-user \
    --conversation-id CONV_ID --user-id USER_ID
```

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "Show me my recent conversations" | `list` |
| "What did we talk about with John?" | `search --query "John"` |
| "Show me the conversation about invoices" | `search --query "invoice"` |
| "Send a message to user_042" | `send --user-id user_042 --content "..."` |
| "Reply to this conversation" | `send-to --conversation-id ... --content "..."` |
| "Start a group chat with Alice and Bob" | `create --participant-ids '["alice_id", "bob_id"]' --title "..."` |
| "Add Carol to this conversation" | `add-user --conversation-id ... --user-id carol_id` |

## Notes

- Conversations are sorted by `last_updated` descending — most recent first
- `send --user-id` will find an existing 1:1 conversation or create a new one
- Message timestamps are Unix timestamps
- `--participant-ids` and similar list arguments take JSON arrays

For example the Hale Nalu engagement notes from October and November mention irrigation repairs and planting schedules, but I know from the project records there were at least two on-site meetings with Darren Oshiro during that period. There's no meeting context in the notes beyond what was planted.\n\nSame with the Turtle Bay notes from the winter prep walkthrough. The entry mentions heliconia replacement but nothing about the conversation with Karen Lee regarding the holiday timeline or the event prep request.\n\nAre there additional notes that didn't make it into the export? I want to make sure we have a complete picture for the report.\n\nVernon",
    "cc": [],
    "parent_id": "664c2d3d290eb999c4833395",
    "attachments": {},
    "timestamp": "2025-12-19T20:15:00Z",
    "is_read": true
  },

arguments.content:
# Turtle Bay Resort — Fieldwork Report
## Landscaping Makeup Visit: March 14, 2026

**Report prepared:** July 10, 2026
**Visit type:** Scheduled makeup landscaping visit
**Site:** Turtle Bay Resort, 57-091 Kamehameha Highway, Kahuku, HI 96731
**Client contact:** Karen Lee, Facilities Director — (808) 555-7815
**Service provider:** Pacific Greenscapes
**Coordinator:** Vernon Arakaki
**Visit date:** Saturday, March 14, 2026

---

## 1. Visit Timeline

| Date/Time | Event | Source |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| Sun 3/8 | Vernon emails Lucia requesting her crew for Sat 3/14, 7:00 AM–2:30 PM. Work scope: heliconia bed cleanup (postponed) and north-lawn irrigation check. Paloma CC'd. | Email: `5b8110214283432a930be0b3` |
| Sun 3/8 (evening) | Lucia asks Vernon whether this is for both crews or just hers, and whether the date is flexible. | Email: `6151dd5a24bcaa62261869f2` |
| Mon 3/9 | Vernon replies: Lucia's crew specifically; resort wants continuity; March 14 is fixed. | Email: `80d68232050cb06ae3b2ac91` |
| Thu 3/12 5:00 PM | Lucia declines — prior commitment she cannot move. Suggests Paloma's crew, noting they have Turtle Bay experience. | Email: `a614997904b99d828db013b9` |
| Thu 3/12 7:30 PM | Vernon acknowledges; says he will check with Paloma. Asks for more lead time on future Saturday conflicts. | Email: `d8e1d4c2b353590f9b36e28a` |
| Thu 3/12 | Crew-change memo written (handwritten): confirms Paloma covering with 3 workers total; notes schedule adjusted to 7:00 AM–2:30 PM; records that N-3 was "still leaking after test" with "temporary tightening only" and "need permanent repair." | `crew_update_0314.jpg` |
| Sat 3/14 7:05 AM | Paloma + 2 workers arrive on-site. | `field_notes_0314.jpg` |
| Sat 3/14 AM | North entrance heliconia maintenance performed. | `field_notes_0314.jpg` |
| Sat 3/14 AM–Early PM | North lawn irrigation inspection; valve N-3 leak identified; 8-minute test; temporary tightening applied. | `field_notes_0314.jpg` |
| Sat 3/14 2:20 PM | Crew departs. | `field_notes_0314.jpg` |

**Note on the crossed-out "8:00 AM" in `crew_update_0314.jpg`:** The handwritten memo shows "8:00 AM" struck through and replaced with "7:00 AM." Vernon's original email (3/8) requested 7:00 AM from the outset. The struck-through entry appears to be a transcription error corrected on the memo itself; it does not represent an official earlier schedule.

---

## 2. Crew Reconciliation

| Detail | Requested | Actual |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| Crew lead | Lucia Gutierrez | Paloma Reyes (substitution confirmed via email 3/12) |
| Workers | Not specified | Paloma + 2 (3 total) |
| Start time | 7:00 AM | 7:05 AM (+5 min) |
| End time | 2:30 PM | 2:20 PM (−10 min) |

**Substitution chain:** Vernon requested Lucia's crew for continuity. Lucia declined Thursday 3/12 citing a prior commitment and recommended Paloma. Vernon confirmed he would check with Paloma. Both handwritten documents confirm Paloma's crew attended.

**Calendar record:** Event `2980c3c2329bb32e2ed95395` — "Crew - Turtle Bay (Saturday)" on 3/14, described as "Makeup day, heliconia beds and irrigation," lists Lucia Gutierrez as attendee. This calendar entry was not updated to reflect the crew substitution.

---

## 3. CRM History — North Entrance Heliconia

The following CRM engagement records establish the planting and maintenance history for the north entrance heliconia bed. These records predate the March 14 visit and are presented separately from the visit-day observations.

| Date | Type | Record | Key Detail |
|------|------|--------|------------|
| Sep 15, 2025 | Meeting | `92632938ba8160d13c925992` — "Winter prep walkthrough" | On-site walkthrough with Karen Lee. North entrance spent ginger to be replaced with yellow heliconia. Oceanfront path cleaning and mulching scheduled before holiday rush. |
| Nov 25, 2025 | Note | `bedb2f638cdac37bad067e2e` — "Year-end property walkthrough" | Yellow heliconia from winter refresh is starting to bloom. Karen Lee described as "thrilled." |
| Jun 15, 2025 | Call | `ebaceb17baa68f6ca8b7445a` — "Summer season check-in" | Heliconia near the north entrance "needs thinning before it overtakes the walkway." |

**Chronological note on the June 2025 call:** The CRM record dated June 15, 2025 refers to heliconia already needing thinning. However, the September 2025 walkthrough describes the north entrance as having *spent ginger* to be *replaced* with yellow heliconia. If both records are accurate, the June entry may refer to an earlier heliconia planting in a different bed, or the records may reflect an evolving plan. This report does not resolve the discrepancy but notes it for completeness.

**March 14 connection:** The heliconia bed cleanup Vernon referenced in his 3/8 email as "postponed" work aligns with this CRM history — the yellow heliconia planted in late 2025 had grown to overtake the walkway and required thinning and spent-stem removal.

---

## 4. Heliconia Work — March 14 Observations

Per the field notes (`field_notes_0314.jpg`):

- **Spent stems removed:** 21 (initially recorded as 22, then corrected to 21)
- **Plants leaning toward path:** 4 identified
- **Path clearance before work:** 22 inches
- **Path clearance after work:** 38 inches
- **Net clearance gained:** 16 inches

`IMG_6148.JPG` shows a heliconia inflorescence consistent with *Heliconia psittacorum* or a 'Golden Torch' cultivar — erect golden-yellow bracts with minor brown spotting on lower bracts. A white wall/structure is visible in the background. This is consistent with the described north entrance work but contains no date stamp, GPS data, or site signage to independently confirm the location. See §7 Evidence Limitations.

---

## 5. Irrigation Findings — North Lawn, Valve N-3

### From `crew_update_0314.jpg` (crew-change memo):
- N-3 "still leaking after test"
- "Temporary tightening only"
- "Need permanent repair"

### From `field_notes_0314.jpg` (field log):
- Leak found near valve N-3
- Test duration: 8 minutes
- Wet area: approximately 3 feet
- Reference number **3879** circled — purpose unidentified (may be a parts number, work-order reference, or internal code; no corroborating record was found in email, calendar, CRM, or messaging data)
- **Clamp replacement recommended**, followed by retest

### Photographic evidence:
- `IMG_6159.JPG` — Active leak spraying from a black irrigation pipe at a compression coupling; metal hose clamp visible. Consistent with the described N-3 temporary tightening and continued leak. No valve label, date stamp, or site marker visible. Classified as **context_only**.
- `IMG_6156.PNG` — Hunter MP Rotator sprinkler head at ground level in reddish-brown laterite soil. Documents an irrigation component but does not depict the N-3 leak. Classified as **context_only**.
- `IMG_6162.JPG` — Pop-up sprinkler head actively spraying on green tropical turf. Possibly related to the 8-minute test zone. No location identifiers. Classified as **context_only**.

### Assessment:
**The irrigation problem at valve N-3 was NOT permanently repaired.** Both handwritten documents independently confirm that only a temporary tightening was performed. The field notes specify a corrective action (clamp replacement + retest) that remains outstanding. See `turtle_bay_follow_up.md`.

---

## 6. Extracted Measurements

| Measurement | Value | Source |
|-------------|-------|--------|
| Heliconia spent stems removed | 21 | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Heliconia plants leaning toward path | 4 | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Path clearance, before | 22 inches | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Path clearance, after | 38 inches | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| N-3 leak test duration | 8 minutes | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| N-3 wet area extent | ~3 feet | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Crew arrival | 7:05 AM | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Crew departure | 2:20 PM | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Total crew size | 3 | crew_update_0314.jpg, field_notes_0314.jpg |

---

## 7. Evidence Limitations

### What the evidence supports:
- The visit occurred on Saturday, March 14, 2026. Both handwritten documents specify this date; the email chain and calendar event corroborate it.
- Vernon requested the visit for 7:00 AM–2:30 PM. Paloma's crew substituted for Lucia's after a Thursday crew change.
- Heliconia maintenance was performed at the north entrance with specific measurements recorded.
- The N-3 valve leak was identified, temporarily addressed, and flagged for permanent repair.

### What the evidence does NOT establish:
- **No photograph in the packet contains a date stamp, GPS overlay, or Turtle Bay Resort signage.** Image content cannot independently confirm the visit location or date.
- **Pool/resort images** (`IMG_6140.JPG`) are visually consistent with a Hawaiian oceanfront resort but contain no identifying markers. The edited version (`IMG_6140_edit.JPG`) is a cropped derivative that adds no new evidence.
- **Lawn/hammock image** (`IMG_6142.JPG`) shows a Hawaiian-style coastal property with no identifying features.
- **Heliconia image** (`IMG_6148.JPG`) shows a species consistent with the field notes but has no location context beyond a white wall.
- **Irrigation images** (`IMG_6156.PNG`, `IMG_6159.JPG`, `IMG_6162.JPG`) depict equipment and conditions consistent with the field notes but lack site-specific identifiers.
- The circled reference number **3879** in the field notes has no corroborating record in available email, calendar, CRM, or messaging data. Its meaning is unidentified.

### Excluded files (moved to `excluded_photos/`):
| File | Classification | Reason |
|------|---------------|--------|
| `IMG_6140_edit.JPG` | Visual duplicate | Cropped/edited version of `IMG_6140.JPG`; adds no new evidence |
| `IMG_6142_copy.JPG` | Exact duplicate | Byte-identical to `IMG_6142.JPG` (MD5: `c4e5737de00270611cb37491f103f1f1`) |
| `IMG_5902.JPG` | Unrelated | Shows "Island Palms Hotel, Casa Del Mar Building" in San Diego, CA |
| `IMG_6151.JPG` | Unrelated | Temperate deciduous tree (likely Black Locust) in autumn; no tropical context |
| `IMG_6170.JPG` | Unrelated | "LeVent Restaurant" exterior; Southeast Asian location |

---

## 8. Source Records Summary

### Email thread: "Saturday coverage needed: Turtle Bay, March 14"
- `5b8110214283432a930be0b3` — Vernon → Lucia (CC Paloma), 3/8: Initial request
- `6151dd5a24bcaa62261869f2` — Lucia → Vernon, 3/8: Clarifying questions
- `80d68232050cb06ae3b2ac91` — Vernon → Lucia, 3/9: Lucia's crew specifically, date fixed
- `a614997904b99d828db013b9` — Lucia → Vernon, 3/12: Declines, suggests Paloma
- `d8e1d4c2b353590f9b36e28a` — Vernon → Lucia, 3/12: Acknowledges, will check with Paloma

### Calendar
- `2980c3c2329bb32e2ed95395` — "Crew - Turtle Bay (Saturday)" 3/14, "Makeup day, heliconia beds and irrigation" (attendee listed as Lucia; not updated for crew swap)

### CRM engagements (Turtle Bay Resort — `ef831fc564f7a8d222e0cb71`)
- `92632938ba8160d13c925992` — Sep 15, 2025: Winter prep walkthrough (ginger → heliconia replacement)
- `ebaceb17baa68f6ca8b7445a` — Jun 15, 2025: Summer check-in (heliconia overtaking walkway)
- `bedb2f638cdac37bad067e2e` — Nov 25, 2025: Year-end walkthrough (heliconia blooming)

### CRM contact
- `063bf6966b9ea1fdb5597c94` — Karen Lee, Facilities Director, Turtle Bay Resort

### Messaging
- PG Crew Chat (`1741f6d4ba0734f70e15adea`) — No March 14 visit-specific messages found; last message 3/12 ("on it" from Lucia)

---

## 9. Recommended Follow-Up

1. **Permanent repair of valve N-3:** Schedule clamp replacement per field note recommendation. Reference number **3879** should be investigated internally to determine whether it corresponds to a parts requisition, work order, or other tracking number. Retest after repair to confirm the leak is resolved.
2. **Calendar update:** Update event `2980c3c2329bb32e2ed95395` to reflect that Paloma's crew attended, not Lucia's, to maintain accurate scheduling records.
3. **Heliconia health monitoring:** The minor brown spotting on lower bracts observed in `IMG_6148.JPG` should be monitored. If it progresses, evaluate for pest or fungal treatment.
4. **Photo documentation standards:** Future visits should include date-stamped and geotagged photographs, with at least one image showing visible site signage, to strengthen the evidentiary chain.
5. **CRM chronology review:** The apparent discrepancy between the June 2025 thinning note and the September 2025 ginger-replacement plan should be clarified with Lucia or Karen Lee to ensure CRM records accurately reflect the planting sequence.

See also: `turtle_bay_follow_up.md` for the N-3 irrigation follow-up action item.

# Turtle Bay Resort — Irrigation Follow-Up Action

## Issue

**North Lawn valve N-3 remains actively leaking after the March 14, 2026 makeup visit.** The leak was not permanently repaired; only a temporary tightening of the compression coupling clamp was performed.

## Supporting Evidence

| Source | Type | Key Detail |
|--------|------|-----------|
| `crew_update_0314.jpg` | Handwritten crew-change memo | "N-3 still leaking after test / Temporary tightening only / need permanent repair" |
| `field_notes_0314.jpg` | Handwritten field log | "Leak found near valve N-3 / Test duration: 8 mins / Wet area: 3 ft approx. / **3879** Clamp replacement recommended and then retest" |
| `IMG_6159.JPG` | Photograph (context_only) | Active water spray from a black irrigation pipe at a compression coupling with a metal hose clamp — consistent with the described temporary tightening and continued leak. No valve label, date stamp, or site marker visible; cannot independently confirm this is N-3 at Turtle Bay. |
| Email `5b8110214283432a930be0b3` | Vernon → Lucia, 3/8 | Original visit scope included "the irrigation check on the north lawn," confirming this was a planned inspection item. |

Both handwritten documents independently confirm the leak persisted after the crew's intervention. The field notes specify a quantified test (8 minutes, ~3 ft wet area) and a specific corrective action (clamp replacement + retest).

## Nature of Previous Action

**Temporary.** The crew-change memo explicitly states "temporary tightening only." The field notes recommend "clamp replacement … and then retest," confirming the on-site action was an interim measure, not a permanent fix.

## Reference Number 3879

The field notes contain a circled reference **3879** adjacent to the clamp-replacement recommendation. No corroborating record was found in the available email, calendar, CRM, or messaging data. Its purpose (parts requisition, work-order number, or other internal code) should be verified with the crew or Pacific Greenscapes operations.

## Recommended Next Action

1. **Schedule a return visit** to replace the compression coupling clamp at valve N-3. Coordinate with Vernon Arakaki and confirm whether reference number 3879 corresponds to an existing parts order or work request.
2. **Perform a pressurized retest** after clamp replacement — minimum 8 minutes to match the original test duration, with wet-area measurement for comparison against the ~3 ft baseline.
3. **Document the repair** with date-stamped, geotagged photographs showing the valve label/number, the replaced clamp, and dry conditions post-retest.
4. **Assess for secondary damage:** Determine whether the sustained leak caused soil erosion, root saturation, or turf degradation in the ~3 ft wet area around N-3.

## Priority

**Medium-high.** The leak is contained to an approximately 3-foot area and was temporarily mitigated, but continued water loss and potential soil/turf damage make a permanent repair time-sensitive. The temporary clamp tightening may degrade further under system pressure.

## Cross-References

- Full visit report: `turtle_bay_fieldwork_report.md` — §5 Irrigation Findings
- Photo log: `turtlr_bay_photo_log.csv` — see entries for `IMG_6159.JPG`, `IMG_6156.PNG`, `IMG_6162.JPG`
- Original visit request: Email `5b8110214283432a930be0b3` (Vernon → Lucia, March 8, 2026)
- CRM company record: `ef831fc564f7a8d222e0cb71` — Turtle Bay Resort
- Client contact: Karen Lee, Facilities Director — (808) 555-7815

# Detailed Image Description

## Plant Identification

This image shows a **leguminous tree** — most likely a **Black Locust (Robinia pseudoacacia)** or similar species in the Fabaceae family. Key identifying features:

- **Pinnately compound leaves** with oval/elliptical leaflets arranged along a central rachis (clearly visible on the left side and background)
- The leaflets are smooth-edged (entire margins), rounded to slightly oblong
- The foliage has the characteristic look of a locust or similar deciduous legume tree

## Central Subject

The focal point is a **cluster of bright golden-yellow leaves** hanging pendant from a thin stem in the center of the frame. These yellowed leaves are beginning to senesce (autumnal color change or natural leaf drop), curling slightly and catching warm, likely late-afternoon or golden-hour sunlight. They stand out dramatically against the darker green foliage behind them.

A **second cluster of yellowing leaves** is partially visible in the lower right corner.

## Heliconia

**No heliconia** is present in this image. This is a temperate deciduous tree, not a tropical monocot.

## Other Observations

- **No location markers, dates, or signage** are visible
- **No equipment, irrigation components, valves, or pipes** are present
- **No wet areas** are discernible
- **No paths or walkways** are visible
- **No measurements** or scale references are included
- The **background** is entirely filled with layered foliage — dense canopy with dappled light filtering through
- Some **dried/brown leaf edges** and small brownish twigs are visible among the green leaves, suggesting seasonal transition (early autumn) or minor stress
- The **lighting** is warm and directional, suggesting late-day sun illuminating the yellow leaves from behind/beside, creating a luminous glow

# Detailed Image Description

## Setting & Location
This appears to be a **tropical oceanfront property**, likely in **Hawaii** (possibly the North Shore of Oahu or Kauai) based on the vegetation, ocean conditions, and overall aesthetic. No specific signage, location markers, or dates are visible in the image.

## Ocean & Background
- The **Pacific Ocean** fills the middle-to-upper background with deep blue water and visible **white-capped waves** breaking offshore, suggesting moderate surf conditions.
- A partly cloudy sky with white cumulus clouds sits above the horizon.
- A **structure/building** is partially visible on the far right edge, with what appears to be a window or glass panel.

## Trees
- **Ironwood trees (Casuarina equisetifolia)** — Several tall, mature specimens dominate the scene with their characteristic drooping, needle-like foliage and dark fissured bark. These are the primary canopy trees, casting long shadows across the lawn.
- **Coconut palm (Cocos nucifera)** — At least one is visible on the right side, identifiable by its smooth, slightly curved trunk and frond silhouette.
- The trees are spaced roughly **10–15 feet apart**, creating a park-like grove.

## Ground Cover & Lawn
- A **well-maintained, lush green turf lawn** covers the ground — likely **Bermuda grass or Zoysia**, mowed short and healthy, indicating regular irrigation and maintenance.
- The lawn appears **uniformly green with no visible dry patches**, suggesting an active irrigation system, though **no irrigation components, sprinkler heads, valves, or wet areas** are directly visible.
- A **landscape edging or border** is faintly visible at the bottom-right corner, possibly brick or stone curbing.

## Shrubs & Plants
- **Naupaka (Scaevola taccada)** — A thick hedgerow of this classic Hawaiian coastal shrub runs along the oceanfront edge, with its characteristic rounded, glossy green leaves. This serves as a windbreak/buffer between the lawn and the ocean.
- **Lower-left foreground:** A **broad-leafed shrub**, possibly **Naupaka** or a **citrus plant** (such as a lemon or lime), with thick, waxy, dark green leaves.
- **No heliconia** plants are visible in this image.
- No plumeria, bird of paradise, or other ornamental tropical flowers are discernible.

## Equipment & Hammock
- A **freestanding hammock** with a **dark brown/wooden curved frame** (arc-style stand) is positioned centrally on the lawn, strung between the two ends of the stand. It appears to be a **rope or woven mesh style** hammock in a brown/natural tone.
- **No irrigation equipment, valves, hose bibs, controllers, or pipe** are visible.
- **No tools, garden equipment, or maintenance gear** are present.

## Paths & Measurements
- **No formal paths, walkways, or stepping stones** are visible — the lawn appears to be an open, continuous area.
- The lawn area visible appears to be roughly **30–40 feet wide** and extends perhaps **40–50 feet** from the foreground to the ocean-side hedge.
- The ironwood trees appear to be **40–60 feet tall** based on scale relative to the hammock and structure.

## Wet Areas
- **No visible wet areas, puddles, standing water, or saturated zones** on the lawn surface.

## Lighting & Time of Day
- The **warm, golden-angled sunlight** and **long shadows** cast to the right suggest this was taken in the **late afternoon** (roughly 3–5 PM), with the sun positioned to the left/west of the frame.

## Summary of Absent Items
- No signage, dates, or location markers
- No heliconia
- No visible irrigation components or valves
- No wet areas
- No formal paths
- No measurement references or scale indicators

content.text:
## Image 1: Heliconia Flower Close-Up

This image shows a **Heliconia** inflorescence in vibrant golden-yellow/orange color. The species appears to be **Heliconia psittacorum** or possibly **Heliconia 'Golden Torch'**, based on the erect, upright orientation of the inflorescence and the slender, pointed bracts.

**Plant details:**
- The inflorescence features multiple alternating bracts arranged along an upright stem, each bract tapering to a sharp point
- The color is a warm golden-yellow to orange, with some minor brown spotting/blemishing on the lower bracts, which could indicate age or minor pest/fungal issues
- Surrounding the flower are large, broad, paddle-shaped **Musaceae-type leaves** (banana-like) with prominent parallel venation, characteristic of Heliconia foliage
- The leaves are a healthy deep green color

**Background:** A pale/white wall or structure is visible behind the plant, suggesting this is planted in a landscaped garden bed near a building. No signage, dates, or location markers are visible.

---

## Image 2: Irrigation Sprinkler Head (MP Rotator)

This is a **top-down close-up** of a **Hunter MP Rotator** irrigation nozzle installed at ground level.

**Equipment details:**
- The nozzle is clearly labeled **"MP ROTATOR"** on the red/salmon-colored ring around the center
- This is a **multi-stream, multi-trajectory rotating nozzle** designed for efficient, low-precipitation-rate irrigation
- The body is **dark blue/black plastic** with radiating fins/ribs around the perimeter
- A **silver/metallic retaining ring** sits between the red indicator ring and the outer housing
- A **center screw** (adjustment screw) is visible at the top center, used for radius adjustment
- The soil around the sprinkler head is **reddish-brown/laterite clay soil**, appearing relatively dry with some loose clumps and minor organic debris
- The sprinkler head is flush with the soil surface, typical of a pop-up spray body installation

**No visible dates, signage, or location markers.** The red-colored soil could suggest a tropical or subtropical location (lateritic soils common in tropical regions), which would be consistent with the heliconia planting seen in Image 1.

content.text:
# Detailed Image Descriptions

## Image 1: Leaking Pipe/Irrigation Connection

**What it shows:** A close-up photograph of a black irrigation pipe (appears to be HDPE or PVC) with a **leak** actively spraying water upward from a joint/connection point. The water is captured mid-spray, with droplets frozen in the air.

**Irrigation Components Visible:**
- A **black mainline pipe** running horizontally across the frame
- A **compression coupling/fitting** (the ribbed black connector on the right side) joining two pipe sections
- A **metal hose clamp** (silver/stainless steel) on the left side of the coupling
- What appears to be a **valve or fitting** (dark/black) on the far right
- A **wire or cable** (possibly a support wire or guy wire) visible in the lower right corner

**Leak Details:** Water is spraying upward forcefully from the connection point between the pipe and the coupling, suggesting a failed seal or improperly tightened fitting. Water also drips downward below the pipe.

**Background:** Dense, blurred green vegetation — appears to be tall grasses or reeds. The overall tone is dark, moody green.

**What is NOT visible:** No signage, no location markers, no dates, no heliconia plants, no paths, no measurements, no valves with handles (though there may be a fitting on the right side). No people, no labels on equipment.

---

## Image 2: Pop-Up Sprinkler Head in Operation

**What it shows:** A ground-level close-up of a **pop-up irrigation sprinkler head** actively spraying water in a fan/cone pattern across a green lawn.

**Irrigation Components Visible:**
- A **pop-up spray head** (appears to be a fixed-pattern type, not a rotor) — the nozzle is small, dark-colored (brown or black), protruding just slightly above grass level
- The spray pattern is a **full or near-full circle fan spray**, creating a fine mist/spray cone
- The grass around the sprinkler is visibly **wet**, with water droplets on the blades

**Vegetation:**
- The lawn appears to be a **tropical grass species** — wide-bladed, lush, and green (possibly St. Augustine, Bermuda, or a tropical turf variety)
- In the **blurred background**, there are **trees with whitewashed/lime-painted trunks** (a common practice in tropical/Southeast Asian landscaping to protect against insects)
- Additional green landscaping and what may be palms are visible in the soft background

**Wet Areas:** The entire foreground lawn is glistening with water from the sprinkler.

**What is NOT visible:** No heliconia, no pipe runs, no valves, no signage, no dates, no location markers, no paths, no measurements, no people.

---

## Image 3: LeVent Restaurant Exterior Signage

**What it shows:** The exterior facade of **"LeVent Restaurant"** — a building with tropical landscaping.

**Signage:**
- **"LeVent"** in large teal/dark green 3D lettering mounted on a white wall
- **"RESTAURANT"** in smaller capital letters beneath
- A **logo/icon** to the upper left of the text — appears to be a stylized paper airplane or wind motif (fitting the French word "Le Vent" meaning "The Wind")

**Architecture:**
- White-painted stucco or masonry wall
- **Dark brown/gray clay tile roof** in a traditional style (possibly Southeast Asian — Vietnamese or Indonesian style)
- A **gray structure/tower** rising above the roofline (possibly an elevator shaft, ventilation unit, or upper-level extension) with what appears to be a glass canopy
- Roof has a hip-style configuration

**Plants/Vegetation:**
- **Right side:** A large **traveler's palm (Ravenala madagascariensis)** or possibly a **banana plant** — large, paddle-shaped leaves fanning out
- **Left side:** A **plumeria/frangipani** tree (visible white flowers at top) and other leafy shrubs
- **Foreground:** Low ornamental shrubs/hedging
- A **potted plant** in a blue-gray ceramic pot on the right side at ground level
- **Grass** visible at bottom right

**What is NOT visible:** No heliconia plants, no irrigation components, no valves, no wet areas, no paths (though there appears to be a paved edge at the far right), no dates, no measurements, no other signage. No people.

**Location clues:** The architectural style (tile roof, tropical landscaping, whitewashed aesthetic) and the French name suggest this could be in **Vietnam** or another Southeast Asian country with French colonial influence.

arguments.command:
cd /root/.openclaw/workspace/inputs && python3 -c "
import hashlib, os
for f in sorted(os.listdir('.')):
    if f.endswith(('.JPG','.jpg','.PNG','.png')):
        h = hashlib.md5(open(f,'rb').read()).hexdigest()
        print(f'{f}: {h} ({os.path.getsize(f)})')
"

---

## 1. Visit Timeline

| Prior week | Lucia crew originally scheduled 8:00 AM – 2:30 PM |
| Thursday 3/12 | Schedule update: start moved to 7:00 AM; Paloma crew assigned to cover (3 workers total) |
| 7:05 AM Sat 3/14 | Paloma + 2 workers arrive on-site |
| AM | North entrance heliconia maintenance performed |
| AM–Early PM | North lawn irrigation inspection and temporary repair of valve N-3 |
| 2:20 PM | Crew departs |

**Total on-site duration:** Approximately 7 hours 15 minutes.

---

## 2. Crew Reconciliation

| Detail | Original Plan | Actual |
|--------|--------------|--------|
| Crew lead | Lucia | Paloma (substitution per Thursday update) |
| Workers | Not specified | Paloma + 2 (3 total) |
| Start time | 8:00 AM (revised to 7:00 AM) | 7:05 AM |
| End time | 2:30 PM | 2:20 PM |

**Source documents:** `crew_update_0314.jpg` (handwritten crew-change memo) and `field_notes_0314.jpg` (handwritten field log).

The crew change from Lucia to Paloma was communicated Thursday, March 12. Both handwritten documents are internally consistent: they agree on the Paloma crew, the 3-worker count, the Saturday 3/14 date, and the North Lawn N-3 irrigation issue. The field notes record an arrival of 7:05 AM (5 minutes after the revised 7:00 AM start), and departure at 2:20 PM (10 minutes early relative to the 2:30 PM end).

---

## 3. Heliconia Work — North Entrance

Per the field notes (`field_notes_0314.jpg`):

- **Spent stems removed:** 21 (initially recorded as 22, then corrected to 21)
- **Plants leaning toward path:** 4 identified
- **Path clearance before work:** 22 inches
- **Path clearance after work:** 38 inches
- **Net clearance gained:** 16 inches

The heliconia species at the resort is consistent with *Heliconia psittacorum* or a 'Golden Torch' variety, based on the erect inflorescence and golden-yellow bracts visible in `IMG_6148.JPG`. Minor brown spotting on lower bracts was observed in that image, which may indicate age or minor pest/fungal stress.

---

## 4. Irrigation Findings — North Lawn, Valve N-3

### Crew-change memo (`crew_update_0314.jpg`):
- N-3 was "still leaking after test"
- Only a "temporary tightening" was performed
- A "permanent repair" is noted as needed

### Field notes (`field_notes_0314.jpg`):
- Leak found near valve N-3
- Test duration: 8 minutes
- Wet area: approximately 3 feet
- Reference number **3879** circled — likely a parts requisition or work-order number
- **Clamp replacement recommended**, followed by retest

### Photographic evidence:
- `IMG_6159.JPG` shows an actively leaking black irrigation pipe at a compression coupling/fitting, with water spraying upward from a failed seal. A metal hose clamp is visible. This is consistent with the described N-3 leak and temporary clamp tightening.
- `IMG_6156.PNG` shows a Hunter MP Rotator sprinkler head at ground level in reddish-brown laterite soil. The soil appears dry. This documents an irrigation component in the system but does not directly depict the N-3 leak.
- `IMG_6162.JPG` shows a pop-up sprinkler head actively spraying on a green lawn, demonstrating an operational zone — possibly used during the 8-minute test referenced in the notes.

### Assessment:
**The irrigation problem at valve N-3 was NOT fully repaired.** Both handwritten documents independently confirm that only a temporary tightening was performed and that a permanent clamp replacement and retest are still required. The active leak photo (`IMG_6159.JPG`) is consistent with a coupling that has been tightened but not properly sealed.

---

## 5. Extracted Measurements

| Measurement | Value | Source |
|-------------|-------|--------|
| Heliconia spent stems removed | 21 | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Heliconia plants leaning toward path | 4 | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Path clearance, before | 22 inches | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Path clearance, after | 38 inches | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| N-3 leak test duration | 8 minutes | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| N-3 wet area extent | ~3 feet | field_notes_0314.jpg |
| Crew on-site duration | ~7 hr 15 min | field_notes_0314.jpg (7:05 AM – 2:20 PM) |
| Total crew size | 3 | crew_update_0314.jpg, field_notes_0314.jpg |

---

## 6. Historical Context

This visit was a **makeup visit** — implying a prior missed or rescheduled service. The crew substitution (Lucia → Paloma) was decided Thursday, two days before the Saturday visit, suggesting an availability or staffing change. The original schedule was also adjusted from an 8:00 AM to a 7:00 AM start, possibly to accommodate the earlier crew or the scope of work.

The N-3 valve issue may predate this visit; the crew-change memo notes it was "still leaking after test," which could imply the leak was a known condition being re-evaluated, not a new discovery. The field notes' recommendation for clamp replacement and retest, along with the circled reference number 3879, suggests a formal follow-up work order was initiated or referenced.

---

## 7. Evidence Limitations

### What the evidence supports:
- The visit occurred on March 14, 2026 (both handwritten documents agree on date)
- Paloma's crew of 3 performed heliconia maintenance and irrigation inspection
- The N-3 valve leak was identified, temporarily addressed, and flagged for permanent repair
- Specific measurements were recorded for path clearance and leak extent

### What the evidence does NOT definitively establish:
- **No photo has a visible date stamp, GPS overlay, or Turtle Bay signage.** The resort pool images (`IMG_6140.JPG`, `IMG_6140_edit.JPG`) are visually consistent with a Hawaiian oceanfront resort but cannot be confirmed as Turtle Bay from image content alone.
- **The lawn/hammock photos** (`IMG_6142.JPG`, `IMG_6142_copy.JPG`) show a Hawaiian-style coastal property but contain no identifying markers.
- **The heliconia photo** (`IMG_6148.JPG`) shows a species consistent with the field notes but has no location context beyond a white wall background.
- **The irrigation photos** (`IMG_6156.PNG`, `IMG_6159.JPG`, `IMG_6162.JPG`) show relevant equipment and an active leak but lack location-specific identifiers. They are consistent with the described conditions but cannot independently confirm the site.
- **IMG_6140_edit.JPG** is a cropped/edited version of the `IMG_6140.JPG` pool scene — it provides no additional evidence beyond the original.

### Excluded files:
- **IMG_5902.JPG** — Shows "Island Palms Hotel, Casa Del Mar Building" (San Diego). This is a different property and is **unrelated** to Turtle Bay Resort.
- **IMG_6142_copy.JPG** — Byte-identical duplicate of `IMG_6142.JPG` (MD5: `c4e5737de00270611cb37491f103f1f1`).
- **IMG_6170.JPG** — Shows "LeVent Restaurant" exterior, a Southeast Asian establishment. **Unrelated** to Turtle Bay Resort.
- **IMG_6151.JPG** — Shows a temperate deciduous tree (likely Black Locust) with yellowing autumn leaves. No tropical or resort context. **Unrelated** to Turtle Bay Resort.

These four files have been moved to `excluded_photos/`.

---

## 8. Recommended Follow-Up

1. **Permanent repair of valve N-3:** Schedule clamp replacement per field note recommendation. Reference work-order/parts number **3879**. Retest after repair to confirm the leak is fully resolved.
2. **Verify makeup visit completion:** Confirm with Paloma or crew whether all originally scoped tasks (beyond heliconia and irrigation) were completed, or whether additional makeup work remains.
3. **Heliconia health check:** The minor brown spotting on lower bracts observed in `IMG_6148.JPG` should be monitored. If it progresses, evaluate for pest or fungal treatment.
4. **Photo documentation improvement:** Future visits should include date-stamped and geotagged photos, and at least one image with visible site signage to strengthen the evidentiary chain.

See also: `turtle_bay_follow_up.md` for the irrigation follow-up action item.

# Turtle Bay Resort — Irrigation Follow-Up Action

## Issue

**North Lawn valve N-3 remains actively leaking after the March 14, 2026 visit.** The leak was not permanently repaired; only a temporary tightening of the compression coupling clamp was performed.

## Supporting Evidence

| Source | Key Detail |
|--------|-----------|
| `crew_update_0314.jpg` | "N-3 still leaking after test / Temporary tightening only / need permanent repair" |
| `field_notes_0314.jpg` | "Leak found near valve N-3 / Test duration: 8 mins / Wet area: 3 ft approx. / **3879** Clamp replacement recommended and then retest" |
| `IMG_6159.JPG` | Photograph showing active water spray from a black irrigation pipe at a compression coupling with a metal hose clamp — consistent with the described temporary tightening and continued leak (no location/date markers in image) |

Both handwritten documents independently confirm the leak persisted after the crew's intervention. The field notes specify a quantified test (8 minutes, ~3 ft wet area) and a specific corrective action (clamp replacement + retest).

## Nature of Previous Action

**Temporary.** The crew-change memo explicitly states "temporary tightening only" and the field notes recommend "clamp replacement … and then retest," confirming the on-site action was an interim measure, not a permanent fix.

## Recommended Next Action

1. **Schedule a return visit** to replace the compression coupling clamp at valve N-3. Reference parts/work-order number **3879** (circled in field notes).
2. **Perform a pressurized retest** after clamp replacement — minimum 8 minutes to match the original test duration, with wet-area measurement for comparison against the 3 ft baseline.
3. **Document the repair** with date-stamped, geotagged photographs showing the valve label/number, the replaced clamp, and dry conditions post-retest.
4. **Assess for secondary damage:** Determine whether the sustained leak caused soil erosion, root saturation, or turf degradation in the ~3 ft wet area around N-3.

## Priority

**Medium-high.** The leak is contained to an approximately 3-foot area and was temporarily mitigated, but continued water loss and potential soil/turf damage make a permanent repair time-sensitive. The temporary clamp tightening may degrade further under system pressure.

---
name: email
description: Read, send, reply, forward, search, and manage emails across inbox, sent, draft, and trash folders. Use when the user asks about emails, sending messages, checking inbox, or email communication.
---

# Email

Read, send, reply to, forward, search, and organize emails. Data is pre-loaded. No email account setup needed.

## Reading Emails

```bash
# List inbox emails (paginated)
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py list --folder INBOX --offset 0 --limit 10

# List sent emails
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py list --folder SENT --limit 20

# Get a specific email by ID
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py get --id abc123def456

# Get an email by its position in a folder
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py get-by-index --index 0 --folder INBOX
```

### Folders

| Folder | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `INBOX` | Received emails (default) |
| `SENT` | Sent emails |
| `DRAFT` | Draft emails |
| `TRASH` | Deleted emails |

## Sending Emails

```bash
# Send a new email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py send --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["tenant@example.com"]' \
    --subject "Rent Reminder" \
    --content "This is a reminder that rent is due on the 1st."

# Send with CC
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py send --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["tenant@example.com"]' \
    --cc '["manager@example.com"]' \
    --subject "Lease Update" \
    --content "Please review the updated terms."

# Send with attachments (pass workspace file paths as a JSON array)
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py send --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["registration@example.com"]' \
    --subject "Registration" \
    --content "Photos attached." \
    --attachment-paths '["IMG_0037.JPEG", "photo-6631.png"]'
```

## Replying & Forwarding

```bash
# Reply to an email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py reply --id abc123 --sender "me@example.com" \
    --content "Thank you for reporting this. We will address it shortly."

# Forward an email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py forward --id abc123 --sender "me@example.com" \
    --recipients '["contractor@example.com"]' \
    --content "Please see the tenant's maintenance request below."
```

## Searching

```bash
# Search emails by keyword (searches subject and content)
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py search --query "plumbing" --folder INBOX --limit 10
```

## Managing Emails

```bash
# Move an email to another folder
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py move --id abc123 --from-folder INBOX --to-folder TRASH

# Delete an email
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py delete --id abc123 --folder INBOX
```

## JMAP & Mailboxes

```bash
# List mailboxes with email counts
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py mailboxes

# Get a specific mailbox
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py mailbox --id INBOX

# List JMAP-formatted emails
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py jmap-list --mailbox-id INBOX --limit 10

# Get JMAP email by ID
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py jmap-get --id abc123

# Get email thread
python3 {baseDir}/email_data.py thread --id thread123
```

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "Check my inbox" | `list --folder INBOX` |
| "Any new emails?" | `list --folder INBOX --limit 5` (check unread) |
| "Read email from Maria" | `search --query "Maria"` then `get --id <id>` |
| "Send a reminder about rent" | `send --sender ... --recipients ... --subject ... --content ...` |
| "Reply to that maintenance email" | `reply --id <id> --sender ... --content ...` |
| "Forward this to the plumber" | `forward --id <id> --sender ... --recipients ... --content ...` |
| "Search for emails about leaking" | `search --query "leaking"` |
| "Delete that spam email" | `delete --id <id> --folder INBOX` |
| "What emails have I sent?" | `list --folder SENT` |
| "How many unread emails?" | `mailboxes` (check unreadEmails count) |

## Notes

- Reading an email by ID or index automatically marks it as read
- Replies auto-populate the `Re:` subject prefix and send to the original sender
- Forwards auto-populate the `Fwd:` subject prefix
- The `sender` field should be the current user's email address

name: calendar
description: View, create, edit, search, and delete calendar events. Filter by date range, tag, or keyword. Use when the user asks about schedules, appointments, events, reminders, or anything time-related.
---

# Calendar

View, create, edit, search, and delete calendar events. Data is pre-loaded. No setup needed.

## Viewing Events

```bash
# Get today's events
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py today

# Get events in a date range
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py range \
    --start "2026-03-01 00:00:00" --end "2026-03-31 23:59:59" \
    --limit 20

# Get a specific event by ID
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py get --id abc123def456

# Show all events (raw data)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py show --offset 0 --limit 100
```

## Filtering by Tag

```bash
# List all tags
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py tags

# Get events by tag
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py by-tag --tag "Maintenance"
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py by-tag --tag "Lease"
```

## Searching Events

```bash
# Search by keyword (searches title, description, location, attendees)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py search --query "plumbing"
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py search --query "Riverside Commons"
```

## Creating Events

```bash
# Create a new event
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py add \
    --title "Maintenance: Fix leaky faucet" \
    --start "2026-03-20 09:00:00" \
    --end "2026-03-20 11:00:00" \
    --tag "Maintenance" \
    --description "Plumber scheduled to fix kitchen faucet in unit 301" \
    --location "Unit 301, Riverside Commons" \
    --attendees '["Mike'\''s Plumbing"]'

# Minimal event (defaults: start=now, end=1hr later)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py add --title "Quick meeting"
```

### Event Fields

| Field | Flag | Required | Format |
|-------|------|----------|--------|
| Title | `--title` | No (defaults to "Event") | Text |
| Start | `--start` | No (defaults to now) | `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"` |
| End | `--end` | No (defaults to start+1hr) | `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"` |
| Tag | `--tag` | No | Text (e.g., "Maintenance", "Lease") |
| Description | `--description` | No | Text |
| Location | `--location` | No | Text |
| Attendees | `--attendees` | No | JSON array: `'["name1", "name2"]'` |

## Editing Events

```bash
# Edit an event (only specified fields are updated)
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py edit --id abc123 \
    --start "2026-03-21 10:00:00" \
    --end "2026-03-21 12:00:00" \
    --description "Rescheduled to Thursday"
```

## Deleting Events

```bash
# Delete an event
python3 {baseDir}/calendar_data.py delete --id abc123
```

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "What's on my calendar today?" | `today` |
| "Any events this week?" | `range --start "YYYY-MM-DD 00:00:00" --end "YYYY-MM-DD 23:59:59"` |
| "Show maintenance events" | `by-tag --tag "Maintenance"` |
| "When is the lease expiring for unit 301?" | `search --query "301"` or `by-tag --tag "Lease"` |
| "Schedule a plumber visit" | `add --title "..." --start "..." --end "..." --tag "Maintenance" ...` |
| "Reschedule the meeting to Thursday" | `edit --id <id> --start "..." --end "..."` |
| "Cancel that appointment" | `delete --id <id>` |
| "What tags are available?" | `tags` |
| "Search for events about Riverside" | `search --query "Riverside"` |

## Notes

- Datetime fields in responses are Unix timestamps (e.g., `1737018000.0`)
- Human-readable versions are in `start_strftime` and `end_strftime` fields
- When creating/editing, always pass datetimes as `"YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS"` strings
- Start time must be before end time

---
name: crm
description: Manage contacts, companies, deals, leads, and engagements via the CRM platform. Use when the user asks about sales pipeline, deals, leads, client relationships, companies, engagement history, or customer relationship management.
---

# CRM

Manage contacts, companies, deals, leads, and engagements. Data is pre-loaded. No API key or setup needed.

## Contacts

```bash
# Search contacts by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-contacts --full-name "Lisa Chen"

# Get a specific contact by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-contact --id CON-1001

# Create a new contact
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-contact --full-name "Jane Doe" --email jane@example.com \
    --company-id COMP-2001 --phone 555-1234 --jobtitle "Marketing Director"

# Delete a contact
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-contact --id CON-1001
```

## Companies

```bash
# Search companies by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-companies --name "Acme"

# Get a specific company by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-company --id COMP-2001

# Create a new company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-company --name "Acme Corp" \
    --industry Technology --domain acme.com --phone 555-0100

# Delete a company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-company --id COMP-2001
```

## Deals

```bash
# Search deals by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-deals --dealname "Chen"

# Search deals by company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-deals --company-id COMP-2001

# Get a specific deal by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-deal --id DEAL-3001

# Create a new deal
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-deal --dealname "Enterprise License" \
    --dealstage qualifiedtobuy --company-id COMP-2001 --amount 50000 \
    --closedate 2026-06-30

# Update deal stage
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py update-deal-stage --id DEAL-3001 --dealstage closedwon

# Delete a deal
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-deal --id DEAL-3001
```

### Deal Stages

| Stage | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `appointmentscheduled` | Initial appointment scheduled |
| `qualifiedtobuy` | Lead qualified as potential buyer |
| `presentationscheduled` | Demo/presentation scheduled |
| `decisionmakerboughtin` | Decision maker approval obtained |
| `contractsent` | Contract sent to customer |
| `closedwon` | Deal won |
| `closedlost` | Deal lost |

## Leads

```bash
# Search leads by name
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-leads --full-name "Smith"

# Search leads by company
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py search-leads --company-id COMP-2001

# Get a specific lead by ID
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py get-lead --id LEAD-4001

# Create a new lead
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-lead --full-name "Bob Smith" --email bob@example.com \
    --company "Acme Corp" --rating hot --leadsource "Website"

# Delete a lead
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-lead --id LEAD-4001
```

### Lead Ratings

| Rating | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `hot` | High priority lead |
| `warm` | Medium priority lead |
| `cold` | Low priority lead |

## Engagements

```bash
# Create an email engagement
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-engagement --engagement-type EMAIL \
    --body "Follow-up on proposal" --contact-ids '["CON-1001"]' --title "Proposal Follow-up"

# Create a call engagement
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-engagement --engagement-type CALL \
    --body "Discussed pricing" --contact-ids '["CON-1001"]' --company-ids '["COMP-2001"]'

# Create a note
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py create-engagement --engagement-type NOTE \
    --body "Client prefers quarterly billing"

# List engagements (optionally filter)
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py list-engagements
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py list-engagements --contact-ids '["CON-1001"]'
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py list-engagements --company-ids '["COMP-2001"]'

# Delete an engagement
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py delete-engagement --id ENG-5001
```

### Engagement Types

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `EMAIL` | Email communication |
| `CALL` | Phone call |
| `NOTE` | Internal note |

## Utility

```bash
# Show raw data
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py show --offset 0 --limit 100

# Reset to initial state
python3 {baseDir}/crm_data.py reset
```

## Data Entities

The CRM system holds:

- **Contacts** -- individual people (id, full_name, email, phone, company_id, jobtitle, address, etc.)
- **Companies** -- organizations (id, name, industry, contact_ids, domain, annualrevenue, numberofemployees, etc.)
- **Deals** -- sales opportunities (id, dealname, dealstage, company_id, amount, closedate, pipeline, etc.)
- **Leads** -- prospective clients (id, full_name, email, company_id, rating, leadsource, notes, etc.)
- **Engagements** -- interaction records (id, engagement_type, body, contact_ids, company_ids, title, etc.)

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "Show me all companies" | `show` or `search-companies --name ""` |
| "Find contact Lisa Chen" | `search-contacts --full-name "Lisa Chen"` |
| "What deals are in the pipeline?" | `search-deals --dealname ""` |
| "Show me the Chen Family deal" | `search-deals --dealname "Chen"` |
| "What's the status of the Alvarez deal?" | `search-deals --dealname "Alvarez"` |
| "Log a call with a client" | `create-engagement --engagement-type CALL --body "..."` |
| "Move deal to closed won" | `update-deal-stage --id DEAL-X --dealstage closedwon` |
| "Who are our hot leads?" | `search-leads` then filter by rating |
| "Show engagement history for Chen" | `list-engagements --company-ids '["COMP-X"]'` |

---
name: messaging
description: Send and receive messages, manage conversations, and search message history. Use when the user asks about messages, conversations, chats, or wants to contact someone.
---

# Messaging

Send messages, browse conversations, and search message history. Data is pre-loaded. No setup needed.

## Viewing Conversations

```bash
# List all conversations (most recently updated first)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py list

# List with pagination
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py list --offset 0 --limit 20

# Get a conversation with its messages
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py get --conversation-id CONV_ID

# Get with message pagination
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py get --conversation-id CONV_ID --offset 0 --limit 50

# Search conversations (searches title, participant names, message content)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py search --query "invoice"

# Show raw data
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py show --offset 0 --limit 100
```

## Sending Messages

```bash
# Send a message to a user (creates a new conversation if none exists)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py send --user-id USER_ID --content "Hello, how are you?"

# Send a message to an existing conversation
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py send-to --conversation-id CONV_ID \
    --content "Following up on the invoice."

# Send with an attachment
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py send --user-id USER_ID \
    --content "Please see the attached report." \
    --attachment "/path/to/report.pdf"
```

## Managing Conversations

```bash
# Create a new conversation with multiple participants
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py create \
    --participant-ids '["user_001", "user_002", "user_003"]' \
    --title "Q1 Budget Review"

# Create without a title (uses participant names)
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py create \
    --participant-ids '["user_001", "user_002"]'

# Add a user to an existing conversation
python3 {baseDir}/messaging_data.py add-user \
    --conversation-id CONV_ID --user-id USER_ID
```

## Answering Questions

| User asks | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| "Show me my recent conversations" | `list` |
| "What did we talk about with John?" | `search --query "John"` |
| "Show me the conversation about invoices" | `search --query "invoice"` |
| "Send a message to user_042" | `send --user-id user_042 --content "..."` |
| "Reply to this conversation" | `send-to --conversation-id ... --content "..."` |
| "Start a group chat with Alice and Bob" | `create --participant-ids '["alice_id", "bob_id"]' --title "..."` |
| "Add Carol to this conversation" | `add-user --conversation-id ... --user-id carol_id` |

## Notes

- Conversations are sorted by `last_updated` descending — most recent first
- `send --user-id` will find an existing 1:1 conversation or create a new one
- Message timestamps are Unix timestamps
- `--participant-ids` and similar list arguments take JSON arrays
